title: Kubernetes Operators and the Valkey Cluster
text:
What This Booklet Covers
Cluster looks calm from a distance.
Requests arrive, pods answer, replicas follow, and the control plane keeps the skyline steady. But the peace is slightly deceptive. This city is held together by memory, routing, trust, and timing. The moment one leader disappears, every hidden system starts speaking at once.
This booklet uses this moment as the entry point, then teaches the system layer by layer.
That means we will cover:
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: Kubernetes Operators and the Valkey Cluster
text:
Kubernetes operators 101
basic operator creation flow
a hands-on basic operator walkthrough with commands and code
ways to build an operator and the tradeoffs behind each path
Helm charts and how they differ from operators
Valkey cluster behavior, including gossip and slot ownership
networking, headless services, and cluster-aware clients
secrets, storage, and node identity
the four paths we explored:

no-operator
OpsTree
our scratch-built operator
the official operator with our merged PVC work and our in-flight shard-aware placement work
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: When the Crown Falls
text:
The Main Components
We start with leader failure because it is the fastest way to see the whole system at once.
Traffic is still flowing. The client still wants answers. Somewhere inside the cluster, replicas are listening, gossip is moving, and the system has to decide whether this is a brief interruption, a real failure, or the beginning of a failover story.
This single scene exposes the roles of Kubernetes, operators, storage, networking, and secrets.
The rest of the booklet keeps returning to three questions:
crumbs: 2  When the Crown Falls

title: When the Crown Falls
text:
Component
Real system role
Why it matters




Kubernetes control plane
API server, controller loops, scheduler, and resource lifecycle management
keeps desired state, scheduling, and resource lifecycles moving


Operator / controller
application-specific reconciliation logic
encodes application-specific behavior and recovery logic


Valkey nodes
running Valkey members
own slots, replication, and runtime cluster state


Valkey cluster bus
node-to-node gossip channel
spreads topology changes and failure signals


PVC / persistent storage
persistent volume claims and attached storage
keeps data and cluster memory alive across pod restarts


nodes.conf
local node identity file
preserves node ID and peer knowledge


Client
application or API client
feels redirects, retries, and outages first


Secrets path
Secrets, API server, etcd, kubelet
controls trust material such as passwords, ACLs, and TLS
crumbs: 2  When the Crown Falls

title: When the Crown Falls
section: Why This Is More Than “Run a StatefulSet”
text:
Why This Is More Than “Run a StatefulSet”
Valkey on Kubernetes is not only about keeping containers alive. It is about preserving the things that make a cluster recognizable to itself:

slot ownership
replica relationships
node identity
stable networking references
trusted credentials

Kubernetes can restart pods and recreate resources, but it does not naturally understand shard ownership, cluster join order, or what it means for a node to return with memory. That is the gap where operators become useful.
crumbs: 2  When the Crown Falls

title: Operators
text:
What the Reconcile Loop Actually Looks Like
Now we discuss what an operator really does, how a basic operator is created, and where Helm charts fit in this picture.
Kubernetes already knows how to keep resources present, restart failed containers, and converge generic object state. What it does not know by itself is how a clustered data system should form, heal, scale, and report readiness. An operator fills that gap by continuously observing real state and reconciling it against the desired one.
flowchart TD
  A["Operator asks API server for CR, status, and child resource state"] --> B["API server serves the current object view"]
  B --> C["Watch event reaches the operator when something changes"]
  C --> D["Operator compares desired state with actual state"]
  D --> E{"Mismatch found?"}
  E -- no --> A
  E -- yes --> F["Operator creates, updates, or deletes resources"]
  F --> G["Operator updates CR status through the API server"]
  G --> H["API server stores the new state in etcd"]
  H --> A
In practice, the operator spends most of its life in this loop:
The important technical point is that the controller watches through the Kubernetes API server. etcd is the backing store, but the controller does not talk to etcd directly.
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Operators
text:
read desired state from the custom resource
inspect current state from Kubernetes and sometimes from the application itself
detect mismatch
reconcile by creating, updating, or cleaning resources
update status so users can see what happened
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Operators
text:
Where Helm Charts Fit
Helm charts are not the same thing as operators, but they are still important in Kubernetes application delivery.
Helm is very good at:

packaging Kubernetes manifests
templating values for different environments
installing and upgrading releases
managing standard objects such as Deployments, StatefulSets, Services, and ConfigMaps

For many applications, a Helm chart is enough. For clustered stateful systems, Helm alone usually stops short when runtime decision-making is needed.








Topic
Helm chart
Operator




main job
package and install manifests
continuously manage application-specific lifecycle


model
render templates and apply them
watch events and reconcile continuously


runtime awareness
limited
strong


application-specific healing
usually manual or external
built into controller logic


best fit
standard app delivery
stateful systems with cluster behavior



An operator can also be packaged and installed with Helm. The difference is that Helm delivers resources, while the operator keeps working after installation.
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Operators
text:
Ways to Create an Operator
There is no single mandatory toolchain. The path depends on how much custom lifecycle logic the workload needs.








Approach
Best when
Tradeoff




Kubebuilder
you want native controller patterns, CRDs, and webhooks
more code, more control


Operator SDK
you want a controller-based operator with packaging support
similar complexity to Kubebuilder, but a more operator-centered project layout


Helm or Ansible operators
the lifecycle is closer to templating and orchestration than deep reconciliation
simpler to begin, but can get tight once runtime logic becomes complex


direct controller code
you want full control over every interaction point
maximum flexibility and maximum ownership burden
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Operators
text:
Which Language Makes Sense
For Kubernetes operators, Go is the strongest default.
The reason is practical rather than ideological:

the Kubernetes ecosystem is Go-first
client-go and controller-runtime are strongest there
most advanced examples, webhooks, and controller patterns are easier to find in Go

Other languages are still possible, but for controller-heavy work with custom reconciliation and admission hooks, Go usually gives the cleanest path.
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Operators
text:
Basic Operator Creation Flow
At a practical level, a simple Go operator usually starts like this:





flowchart TD
  A["kubebuilder init"] --> B["kubebuilder create api"]
  B --> C["Edit api/v1alpha1/valkey_types.go"]
  C --> D["Edit internal/controller/valkey_controller.go"]
  D --> E["make manifests generate fmt"]
  E --> F["make install"]
  F --> G["Run the manager"]
  G --> H["kubectl apply sample CR"]
  H --> I["Controller creates child resources and updates status"]






The important implementation stages are:

define the custom resource shape
create the CRD and API types
write the controller and its reconcile method
set up RBAC, manager wiring, and deployment
add webhooks when validation or mutation is needed
deploy the operator and test it with a real custom resource

The next chapter follows this exact path with the real files and commands.
crumbs: 3  Operators

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
What We Will Build
This chapter shows one concrete operator path that can be followed on a real machine.
To keep the example small, the operator watches a custom resource named Valkey and creates:
This is not a production Valkey cluster operator. It is a small learning operator that shows the real project shape, commands, and controller code behind reconciliation.
The custom resource will look like this:
When this object is applied, the controller will:
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
apiVersion: cache.example.com/v1alpha1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: valkey-sample
spec:
  image: valkey/valkey:8.0
  replicas: 1
  port: 6379


read the Valkey resource
create or update a Service
create or update a Deployment
publish status.phase and status.readyReplicas
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Prerequisites
You need:

go
kubectl
docker
kind
kubebuilder

Install Kubebuilder:
curl -L -o kubebuilder "https://go.kubebuilder.io/dl/latest/$(go env GOOS)/$(go env GOARCH)"
chmod +x kubebuilder
sudo mv kubebuilder /usr/local/bin/
kubebuilder version
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Create a Local Cluster
kind create cluster --name valkey-operator-doc
kubectl cluster-info --context kind-valkey-operator-doc
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Scaffold the Project






Note



Use a lowercase, hyphen-only project directory such as valkey-operator-doc. In local testing, Kubebuilder rejected directory names that used . or _.


mkdir -p ~/projects/valkey-operator-doc
cd ~/projects/valkey-operator-doc

kubebuilder init \
  --domain example.com \
  --repo github.com/example/valkey-operator \
  --plugins=go/v4

kubebuilder create api \
  --group cache \
  --version v1alpha1 \
  --kind Valkey \
  --resource=true \
  --controller=true
The main files created by the scaffold are:

api/v1alpha1/valkey_types.go
internal/controller/valkey_controller.go
config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_valkey.yaml
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Replace the Sample Custom Resource
Edit config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_valkey.yaml to:
apiVersion: cache.example.com/v1alpha1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: valkey-sample
spec:
  image: valkey/valkey:8.0
  replicas: 1
  port: 6379
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Run the Tests
Use the scaffolded test target:
make test






Note



make test downloads the envtest binaries automatically. On a fresh machine, plain go test ./... can fail because the local etcd and kube-apiserver test assets are not installed yet.
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
section: Install the CRD and Run the Controller
text:
Install the CRD and Run the Controller
Install the CRD into the cluster:
make install
Run the controller from another terminal:
cd ~/projects/valkey-operator-doc
make run






Warning



If make run fails with listen tcp :8081: bind: address already in use, run the manager directly with a different probe port:
go run ./cmd/main.go --health-probe-bind-address=:18081
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Apply the Sample Resource
With the controller still running, apply the sample:
kubectl apply -f config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_valkey.yaml
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Verify the Result
Check that the Service and Deployment were created:
kubectl get service valkey-sample
kubectl get deployment valkey-sample
kubectl wait --for=condition=available deployment/valkey-sample --timeout=180s
Then check the custom resource status:
kubectl get valkey valkey-sample -o=jsonpath='{.status.phase}{"\n"}{.status.readyReplicas}{"\n"}'
Expected output:
Ready
1
If you want the full object:
kubectl get valkey valkey-sample -o yaml
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
What This Walkthrough Proved
This walkthrough was validated on a fresh local kind cluster. The end result was:

CRD installed successfully
Valkey sample created successfully
Service created
Deployment created
pod reached Running
status.phase=Ready
status.readyReplicas=1
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code
text:
Cleanup
Delete the sample and remove the cluster when you are done:
kubectl delete -f config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_valkey.yaml
kind delete cluster --name valkey-operator-doc
The important lesson from this chapter is not the size of the controller. It is the full end-to-end chain:

define the API
write reconcile logic
install the CRD
run the manager
apply a CR
watch child resources appear
publish status back to the CR
crumbs: 4  Create a Basic Operator: Commands and Code

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
Why We Needed Multiple Tracks
This section is not theory. These were the real implementation and study tracks we used to understand the problem from different angles.
If we had looked at only one operator, we would have missed too much:
That is why this booklet treats them as workstreams, not as random options in a table.
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
the no-operator path teaches raw mechanics
OpsTree shows what a working production-style operator feels like
our scratch-built operator shows how the control flow changes when we own the CRD, controller, annotations, and webhook
the official operator shows what an upstream architecture looks like when it already has ValkeyCluster and internal ValkeyNode objects
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
Workstream 1: No-Operator
This path kept the mechanics exposed:

manual cluster formation
direct Kubernetes objects
shell-driven operational steps
minimal abstraction hiding what happens

This was useful because it forced us to learn:

what resources really have to exist
how pods come up
what the cluster needs before it can form
where day-two work becomes painful without controller logic
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
Workstream 2: OpsTree
OpsTree gave us a working operator-shaped reference:

cluster CRD
controller-managed resources
StatefulSets
PVC-backed workloads
a more realistic operational lifecycle

This helped us move from raw mechanics to a more practical operator model.
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
Workstream 3: Our Scratch-Built Operator
This track mattered because we moved from studying operators to building one ourselves.
The important parts were:

our own ValkeyCluster CRD
our own controller logic
generated ConfigMap, Service, StatefulSet, and job resources
annotation-based signaling for shard anti-affinity
a mutating webhook that patches the final pod spec before scheduling

This is where the design became concrete, because we had to decide exactly where each policy belonged.
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
section: Workstream 4: Official Valkey Operator Plus Our Changes
text:
Workstream 4: Official Valkey Operator Plus Our Changes
The official operator already had a strong base:

ValkeyCluster
internal ValkeyNode
cluster bootstrap logic
rolling updates
degraded-state recovery
TLS and ACL support
support for both StatefulSet and Deployment

Our work here was not to throw that away. It was to identify the missing production-facing pieces and shape them in a way that fits the existing architecture.
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
Side-by-Side Comparison











Path
Day 1
Day 2
Transparency
Production fit
What it taught us




No-operator
simple to start
heavier to maintain
highest
weaker long-term
forced us to understand every moving part


OpsTree
more setup upfront
calmer operations
medium
strong
showed a practical operator-first workflow


Scratch-built operator
highest ownership burden
flexible if maintained well
high
depends on long-term commitment
let us encode webhook and shard-affinity behavior ourselves


Official operator plus our design direction
good upstream base
stronger if gaps are closed
medium
potentially strongest
showed where PVC handling and shard-safe placement need to grow
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: The Workstreams We Actually Explored
text:
What These Workstreams Taught Us
Together, the four paths gave us a complete picture:

no-operator taught us the raw mechanics
OpsTree showed what a mature operator workflow feels like
the scratch-built path showed where to place our own control hooks
the official path showed where upstream can be extended instead of replaced

The next three chapters go into the technical details of those implementation tracks.
crumbs: 5  The Workstreams We Actually Explored

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
text:
High-Level Control Flow
This chapter describes the work pattern we followed in our own operator and the control shape we learned from the OpsTree-style approach.
OpsTree gave us a practical reference for the overall backbone:
Our scratch-built operator followed that same backbone and added our own annotation and mutating-webhook path for shard-aware anti-affinity.
The key idea is simple:
flowchart TD
  A["User applies ValkeyCluster CR"] --> B["API server validates request"]
  B --> C["ValkeyCluster stored in etcd"]
  C --> D["Controller receives watch event from API server"]
  D --> E["Create ConfigMap, headless Services, client Service, StatefulSets"]
  E --> F{"shardReplicaAntiAffinity = true?"}
  F -- yes --> G["Add anti-affinity annotation to pod template"]
  F -- no --> H["Keep pod template unchanged"]
  G --> I["API server validates and stores child resources"]
  H --> I
  I --> J["StatefulSet controller creates Pod objects"]
  J --> K["API server admission handles Pod create"]
  K --> L{"Annotation present?"}
  L -- yes --> M["Mutating webhook patches shard anti-affinity"]
  L -- no --> N["Pod spec remains unchanged"]
  M --> O["Scheduler assigns node according to anti-affinity and other rules"]
  N --> O
  O --> P["Kubelet starts pod on assigned node"]
  P --> U["CNI plugin assigns IP and routing"]
  U --> Q["Operator-managed bootstrap introduces nodes"]
  Q --> R["Assign slots to primaries"]
  R --> S["Attach replicas to chosen primaries"]
  S --> T["Operator updates cluster status to Ready"]
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
text:
Step-by-Step Runtime Flow
The control flow above becomes easier to follow if we break it into exact stages.

1. The Cluster CR Is Applied

the user applies ValkeyCluster
the API server validates the object against the CRD schema
once valid, the object is persisted in etcd

From the controller point of view, it is watching the API server. The API server is backed by etcd, so this is how the controller learns about the new desired state.


2. The Controller Creates the Base Resources
After the watch event arrives, the controller creates the resources required for the cluster to exist:

ConfigMap
headless Services for stable pod identity
client-facing Service
StatefulSet objects
supporting resources such as PDBs or bootstrap jobs, depending on the operator design

In our scratch-built operator, the StatefulSet pod template is also the place where we attach the signal for shard-aware scheduling.


3. The Annotation Signals the Webhook
If shardReplicaAntiAffinity: true is set on the cluster:

the controller adds an annotation to the pod template
the StatefulSet later creates Pod objects from that template
when the pod create request reaches admission, the webhook sees the annotation
the webhook injects the final anti-affinity rules into the pod spec

This was an important design choice:

the controller decides which pods need special treatment
the webhook decides how the pod spec should be mutated before scheduling
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
section: Why We Used Annotation Plus Webhook Here
text:
Why We Used Annotation Plus Webhook Here
In this operator design, the annotation-plus-webhook path gave us a clean separation:

the controller kept the cluster-level decision logic
the webhook handled the last-mile mutation at pod creation time
the scheduler still received a normal Kubernetes pod spec with anti-affinity applied

That made the scheduling path explicit without hardcoding every anti-affinity rule into a single static manifest.
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
text:
What the Webhook Actually Does
The webhook is not mutating all pods in the namespace. That would be dangerous.
The logic is intentionally narrow:

it only runs for pod create requests
it only targets pods with the cluster label
it only patches pods carrying the shard anti-affinity annotation

Then it injects anti-affinity so a pod is kept away from its paired leader or follower on the same Kubernetes node.
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
section: After Scheduling, the Cluster Still Is Not Formed
text:
After Scheduling, the Cluster Still Is Not Formed
Even once the pods are running, the Valkey cluster does not exist yet in the logical sense.
At this stage, the operator-managed bootstrap flow still has to:

identify which pods are ready
introduce them to each other with CLUSTER MEET
assign slots to the primary nodes
attach replicas to the intended primaries
wait for the replication relationship to settle

Only after that can the cluster be considered truly ready.
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
text:
Bootstrap and Topology Formation
This is the part that turns Kubernetes resources into a real Valkey cluster:

all required pods come up
bootstrap logic discovers their addresses
isolated nodes are introduced into one cluster
primaries receive slot ownership
replicas are attached to their matching primary
the controller checks the resulting topology and status
status is updated to Ready
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
section: What We Learned From This Flow
text:
What We Learned From This Flow
This workstream gave us several concrete lessons:

watching through the API server is one thing; forming the live Valkey cluster is another
a running pod is not the same as a joined cluster member
admission webhooks must be scoped very tightly or they can block the operator itself
shard anti-affinity only works if replica pairing is deterministic enough to match the scheduling policy
nodes.conf, PVCs, and runtime cluster commands all matter after the pod is already running

That is why this workstream was more than a controller exercise. It was where resource reconciliation, admission mutation, scheduling, and live Valkey topology all met in one place.
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow
section: Runtime Issues We Actually Hit While Building It
text:
Runtime Issues We Actually Hit While Building It
The learnings from this operator were not abstract. They came from failures on a real cluster.








Issue
What happened
What we changed




leader election RBAC
the manager could not become leader without leases permissions
added coordination.k8s.io/leases RBAC from day one


webhook self-dependency
the mutating webhook tried to intercept the operator’s own pod creation
scoped the webhook to only mutate cluster pods with the right label


webhook trust
a bad caBundle or serving certificate blocked pod creation
generated the CA and serving certs together and kept one source of truth


PVC defaulting
storageClassName: "" prevented the default storage class from being used
left the field unset when the user did not specify it


PVC binding vs anti-affinity
early node-local PVC binding conflicted with leader-follower anti-affinity and could reintroduce L-F SPOF risk or pod will goes in pending
used a StorageClass with WaitForFirstConsumer so PVC binding happened after scheduler placement, and kept shard anti-affinity enabled


optimistic reconcile
the controller expected new child resources to be readable immediately
treated early NotFound as convergence-in-progress and requeued


deterministic pairing
automatic replica assignment did not always match ordinal assumptions
moved to an explicit bootstrap path where the intended replica relationship is known



Two lessons mattered especially for shard-aware placement:

pod-name-based anti-affinity is only safe when bootstrap guarantees the same pairing model
seeing a seed node in CLUSTER NODES is not enough to prove the full cluster bootstrap finished
crumbs: 6  Scratch-Built Operator and OpsTree-Style Flow

title: Official Valkey Operator
text:
The Two-Level Model
The official operator follows a more layered design than the scratch-built operator. The most important difference is that it uses:
This makes the runtime flow more detailed, but also more explicit.
At a high level:
That means cluster intent and per-node workload management are separated.
crumbs: 7  Official Valkey Operator

title: Official Valkey Operator
text:
ValkeyCluster describes the whole cluster
the operator calculates the topology
it creates one ValkeyNode per required Valkey member
the ValkeyNode controller then creates the actual workload for that member
crumbs: 7  Official Valkey Operator

title: Official Valkey Operator
text:
Runtime Flow, Step by Step

1. CRDs Are Already Installed
Before the user does anything, the cluster already knows the custom resource definitions.
That means Kubernetes understands that these resource kinds exist:

ValkeyCluster
ValkeyNode



2. The User Applies ValkeyCluster
The user submits a cluster-level object that contains settings such as:

shard count
replica count
image
scheduling rules
TLS or ACL configuration



3. The API Server Validates and Stores the Cluster Object
The API server:

validates the object against the CRD schema
stores the object in etcd if valid

At this point, desired state exists in the Kubernetes control plane.


4. The Operator Watches the Cluster Object
The operator is watching the API server for ValkeyCluster changes.
Once the new object appears, reconcile starts.


5. The Operator Creates Cluster-Level Resources
The first resources are cluster-scoped supporting objects, such as:

headless Service
ConfigMap
internal auth or ACL resources

These are again validated by the API server and persisted in etcd.


6. The Operator Calculates Desired Topology
The operator calculates how many Valkey members are needed:
total members = shards × (1 + replicas)
For example:

3 shards
1 replica per shard
total = 6 Valkey members



7. The Operator Creates Internal ValkeyNode Objects
For each required member, the operator creates one ValkeyNode.
These are not Kubernetes worker nodes. They are internal records for the individual Valkey members the cluster should contain.


8. The API Server Stores Those ValkeyNode Objects
Now the topology is represented in more detail:

cluster-level intent in ValkeyCluster
per-member intent in ValkeyNode



9. The ValkeyNode Controller Creates Real Workloads
The ValkeyNode controller reads the internal node objects and creates the real runnable workload for each one:

StatefulSet
or Deployment, depending on the selected workload type

In the common path:

1 ValkeyNode = 1 workload = 1 pod



10. The Scheduler and Kubelet Bring Up the Pods
From here the standard Kubernetes flow takes over:

pod objects are created
the scheduler picks nodes
kubelet pulls images, mounts ConfigMaps and Secrets, and starts containers
pod status is updated through the API server and stored in etcd



11. The Operator Observes Readiness and Pod IPs
Once the pods exist, the operator looks at the runtime information:

which nodes are ready
what pod IP each member has
whether the expected workloads actually came up



12. The Operator Reads Live Valkey State
This is one of the most important parts of the official flow.
At this stage, the operator does not rely only on etcd or Kubernetes object status. It also reads the live Valkey cluster over the network using commands such as:

CLUSTER INFO
CLUSTER NODES

That is how it learns the actual runtime topology.


13. The Operator Forms the Cluster
If nodes are still isolated, the operator introduces them with:

CLUSTER MEET

Then it performs the logical topology setup:

assign slots to primaries
attach replicas to primaries



14. The Operator Verifies Health
It then checks:

expected shard count
expected replica count
slot coverage across all 16384 slots
readiness of the formed cluster



15. The Operator Updates Cluster Status
When those checks pass, the operator updates ValkeyCluster.status and marks the cluster ready.
That status update goes back through the API server and is stored in etcd.


16. The Loop Never Really Stops
This same reconcile structure keeps running for:

scale-out
scale-in
rolling updates
pod restart recovery
topology drift
degraded-state handling
crumbs: 7  Official Valkey Operator

title: Official Valkey Operator
text:
Why This Architecture Matters
The official operator is important because it separates:

cluster-level desired state
internal node-level desired state
workload reconciliation
live cluster observation

That separation is what made it possible for us to think about changes such as persistence and shard-aware placement in a more architecture-friendly way.
crumbs: 7  Official Valkey Operator

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
Upstream Status Snapshot
Once we understood the official operator runtime, the next question was not “can it run at all?” It clearly can.
The question became:
What is still missing for our production use case, and how should those pieces be added without breaking the architecture that is already there?
Two tracks mattered most:
This chapter is not only about design ideas. It is also about what happened when those ideas were taken upstream.
That gives this chapter a useful split:
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
On May 5, 2026, the persistence track merged upstream as PR #149: Add ValkeyNode-managed persistence support.
As of May 11, 2026, the shard-aware placement track is still in review as PR #163: feat: add shard-aware topology spread constraints.



one part of our work is now part of the official operator
one part is still shaping the upstream design conversation
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
Change Track 1: PVC-Backed Persistence
The persistence discussion changed over time. That is important, because the final direction only makes sense after seeing what the earlier approach could and could not do.

First Attempt: StatefulSet volumeClaimTemplates
The earliest persistence experiment attached storage through the StatefulSet path.
That meant:

persistence was tied directly to the StatefulSet workload
the pod mounted storage coming from volumeClaimTemplates
the design was naturally centered around the StatefulSet resource itself

This was a reasonable first step because it is a common Kubernetes pattern for stateful workloads.


Why We Did Not Stop There
We did not continue with the pure volumeClaimTemplates path for several reasons.

1. It was too tightly coupled to one workload path
The official operator architecture is centered around ValkeyNode.
That means the real owner of “this Valkey member needs persistence” is not the raw StatefulSet template. It is the ValkeyNode object that represents that member.


2. It made persistence feel like a workload detail instead of a node-level contract
What we actually wanted was:

cluster spec declares persistence
that spec is propagated to each ValkeyNode
each node manages its own PVC lifecycle

That matches the official architecture much better than hiding persistence only inside a workload template.


3. volumeClaimTemplates are awkward when the storage story has to evolve
Our design requirements were not just “attach a volume once.”
We also cared about:

explicit reclaim behavior
clearer status when PVCs are pending
resize-aware behavior
node-level visibility

That becomes harder when the storage logic is buried inside volumeClaimTemplates, especially when the official operator already has a per-node abstraction and when StatefulSet storage changes need careful handling.


4. We wanted the PVC lifecycle to be explicit and testable
At the node level, we could reason much more clearly about:

when the PVC is created
when it is ready
when it is resized
whether it should be retained or deleted

That is a much cleaner operational story.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: The Direction We Moved To: ValkeyNode-Managed PVCs
text:
The Direction We Moved To: ValkeyNode-Managed PVCs
The design we moved toward was:

add persistence to ValkeyCluster
propagate that persistence block to each ValkeyNode
let the ValkeyNode controller create and manage a named PVC directly
mount that PVC into the workload created for the node
surface PVC readiness and resize state through ValkeyNode.status
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
What Happened Upstream
This persistence direction is no longer just a proposal in our notes.
It landed upstream through PR #149, which means the official operator now has:

cluster-level persistence intent on ValkeyCluster
propagation of that intent into each ValkeyNode
node-managed PVC lifecycle
reclaim behavior
resize-aware status reporting
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: Persistence Flow With the New Design
text:
Persistence Flow With the New Design





flowchart TD
  A["User sets persistence in ValkeyCluster"] --> B["ValkeyCluster controller propagates persistence to each ValkeyNode"]
  B --> C["ValkeyNode controller reconciles PVC for that node"]
  C --> D["PVC created as a first-class child resource"]
  D --> E["Workload mounts named PVC"]
  E --> F["Valkey stores data and nodes.conf on persistent storage"]
  F --> G["ValkeyNode status reports PVC readiness and size state"]
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
What the Implementation Added
The persistence work added several concrete pieces:

a PersistenceSpec
propagation from ValkeyCluster to ValkeyNode
direct PVC creation in the ValkeyNode controller
status conditions for:

PVC ready
PVC size ready
resize in progress
resize infeasible

reclaim policy support:

Retain
Delete

finalizer-driven cleanup when delete behavior is requested
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
Guardrails We Added
The design was not “allow anything.”
We also added guardrails so the operator behaves predictably:

persistence requires StatefulSet
persistence cannot be removed after creation
persistence cannot be added later to an existing node
storage class is immutable
size can only grow, not shrink

These rules came from operational reality, not from theory.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: Why This Matches the Official Architecture Better
text:
Why This Matches the Official Architecture Better
This approach fits the official operator because:

ValkeyCluster stays the cluster-level intent
ValkeyNode remains the owner of per-member state
the ValkeyNode controller remains the place that creates concrete child resources
PVC lifecycle becomes visible and controllable at the same level as workload lifecycle
the design can clearly reject unsupported persistence combinations such as Deployment plus durable node storage

That is why this direction felt stronger than staying with raw StatefulSet claim templates.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
Change Track 2: Shard-Aware Placement
The second important track was placement of Master and its Replica.
The problem is not generic anti-affinity alone. The real problem is this:

a primary and its replica should not quietly land in the same failure domain
the placement policy should understand shard relationships, not just pod labels in general
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: What We Learned From the Scratch Operator
text:
What We Learned From the Scratch Operator
In the scratch-built operator, we explored shard safety through:

an annotation on the pod template
a mutating webhook
final anti-affinity injection during pod admission

That worked well for that architecture because we owned the admission path and the pairing model.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: Why the Official Operator Needed a Different Shape
text:
Why the Official Operator Needed a Different Shape
For the official operator, a webhook-first model was less attractive because:

the operator already has a strong ValkeyNode abstraction
pod specs are already built in the ValkeyNode controller
native Kubernetes scheduling fields fit more naturally into that path

So instead of copying the webhook model directly, we explored a more native approach.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: The Direction We Explored: Topology Spread Constraints
text:
The Direction We Explored: Topology Spread Constraints
The design direction here was:

expose topology spread configuration at cluster level
propagate it to each ValkeyNode
augment the selector with shard-aware labels in the resource builder
let the scheduler enforce spread across the topology domain
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
What Is Happening Upstream Now
The placement track followed a different path.
Our first anti-affinity-oriented PR was a step toward the problem, but the design discussion moved toward a more native Kubernetes model:

use topologySpreadConstraints
keep the API expressive
add shard-awareness by augmenting selectors and label keys
surface scheduling failure clearly when a strict policy cannot be satisfied

That is what the current upstream work in PR #163 represents.
So unlike persistence, placement should be described here as:

an active upstream proposal
already implemented and tested in our branch
still under review as part of the official operator’s future direction

That made the control path cleaner:

the cluster spec declares the placement intent
the ValkeyNode spec carries the per-node version of that intent
the pod template is built with the final topology spread constraints
the scheduler sees a normal native Kubernetes scheduling spec
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
section: Placement Flow With the Official Operator Direction
text:
Placement Flow With the Official Operator Direction





flowchart TD
  A["User sets placement policy on ValkeyCluster"] --> B["ValkeyCluster controller copies policy to ValkeyNode"]
  B --> C["ValkeyNode resource builder augments topology selectors with cluster and shard labels"]
  C --> D["Workload pod template contains final topologySpreadConstraints"]
  D --> E["Scheduler spreads primary and replica pods across topology domains"]
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction
text:
What Is Still Open
The placement design discussion is still evolving, and that should be stated clearly.
What we already have is:

a much better understanding of the right insertion point
code showing how shard-aware topology spread can be generated
a cleaner upstream-friendly direction than a custom webhook dependency

What still needs final agreement is the exact external API and how strict the placement guarantees should be under capacity pressure.
crumbs: 8  Official Operator: Our Changes and Design Direction

title: The Living Cluster
text:
Valkey Cluster Basics
Now we discuss how the Valkey cluster works at runtime: how the client reaches the correct node, how headless services help with identity, how gossip spreads cluster knowledge, and what changes when pods restart.
A Valkey cluster is not just a set of identical pods. Each member has a role and a relationship to the wider topology.
That is why cluster behavior cannot be reduced to “one service in front of many pods.”
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
text:
primaries own hash slots
replicas shadow those primaries and are candidates for promotion
gossip keeps topology and failure knowledge moving between nodes
clients need a current slot map so they can reach the right owner
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
section: How the Client Connects to the Cluster
text:
How the Client Connects to the Cluster
In a real cluster, the client does not blindly send every request to one random node. A cluster-aware client learns the slot map and then targets the correct primary.





sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client or API Layer
  participant S as Startup Node
  participant P as Slot Owner Primary

  C->>S: Initial connection
  S-->>C: Cluster slot map
  C->>C: Cache slot-to-node mapping
  C->>P: Send command to slot owner
  alt topology changed
    P-->>C: Redirect or slot update
    C->>S: Refresh slot map
    C->>P: Retry to new owner
  end






The important point is this:

a service or load balancer can provide the first entry point
but correct request routing depends on slot ownership
after failover, the client must refresh its view and talk to the new owner
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
text:
Normal Service vs Headless Service
For stateful systems, these two service types do different jobs.








Service type
What it gives you
Why it matters here




normal Service
one stable service identity and generic traffic distribution
useful as an entry point


headless Service
pod-specific DNS records
gives peers and controllers a stable way to address individual members



The headless service is especially important for StatefulSets because it gives each pod a stable DNS identity even when pod IPs change (Pod restart).





flowchart TD
  A["StatefulSet creates pod valkey-0"] --> B["Headless Service publishes pod DNS record"]
  B --> C["valkey-0.service.namespace.svc"]
  C --> D["Peers, operator, and diagnostics can target exact pod"]
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
text:
How Gossip Drives Failover
Valkey uses its cluster bus to exchange health and topology information between nodes. This is the gossip protocol that makes runtime failure handling self-contained.
The important point is that the operator may help with cluster formation, but normal leader failure detection and replica promotion happen inside the Valkey cluster itself.





flowchart TD
  A["Nodes exchange PINGs over the cluster bus"] --> B["No response within cluster-node-timeout"]
  B --> C["Node is marked PFAIL in a local view"]
  C --> D["PFAIL is gossiped across primary nodes"]
  D --> E{"Majority agrees the node is down?"}
  E -- no --> A
  E -- yes --> F["Node is marked FAIL"]
  F --> G["Eligible replica starts failover and requests authorization"]
  G --> H{"Majority ACK received?"}
  H -- no --> D
  H -- yes --> I["Replica is promoted to the new primary"]
  I --> J["Cluster stabilizes and clients are redirected"]
  J --> K["Old primary can later rejoin as a replica"]






In short:

Possible Failure (PFAIL) is a local suspicion
FAIL is a cluster-level decision
promotion needs replica candidacy plus cluster agreement
clients see the result through redirects and slot-map refresh, not through the gossip messages directly
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
section: What Happens When a Leader or Follower Falls
text:
What Happens When a Leader or Follower Falls

When a Leader Fails





sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant P as Current Primary
  participant G as Gossip and Primary Consensus
  participant R as Replica

  C->>P: Write request for owned slot
  P--xC: Primary stops responding
  G->>G: Exchange PING and mark PFAIL
  G->>G: Majority confirms FAIL
  R->>G: Start failover and request authorization
  G-->>R: Promotion acknowledged
  R->>R: Become new primary
  C->>R: Refresh slot map or follow redirect






What changes after a leader failure:

failure is first suspected locally, then confirmed by cluster gossip
an eligible replica is promoted to the new primary
slot ownership moves to the promoted replica
clients must refresh routing or follow redirects before writes fully normalize
the old primary can later return, but it usually comes back as a replica

One subtle point from the failover notes is important: failover changes roles, not pod placement. The pods usually stay on the same Kubernetes nodes; what changes is the internal Valkey role attached to each pod.


When a Follower Fails





sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant P as Primary
  participant F as Follower
  participant G as Gossip

  C->>P: Write request
  P->>F: Replication stream
  F--xP: Follower stops responding
  G->>G: Failure is noticed and shared
  C->>P: Client continues writing to the same primary
  P->>P: Cluster runs with reduced redundancy






What changes after a follower failure:

client write routing usually does not change immediately because the primary still owns the slot
replication safety drops because one copy is missing or stale
the shard remains functional, but it is less protected until the follower returns or a replacement is attached
if the follower comes back with persistent identity, rejoin is smoother; without that identity, recovery becomes more manual

So the networking story is inseparable from topology and failover:

the client only sees slot owners and redirects
the cluster uses gossip to decide whether a node is actually down
safe recovery depends on role transitions, storage identity, and physical separation all working together
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
text:
A Pod Without Memory
Two situations lead to the same operational outcome:

a brand new pod joins the cluster for the first time
an old pod restarts without its previous cluster state, usually because nodes.conf and related files were not preserved

In both cases, the cluster sees an unknown member. The pod starts the Valkey process, opens the client and cluster-bus ports, and waits to be introduced.





sequenceDiagram
  participant N as New or Restarted Pod
  participant O as Operator or Script
  participant E as Existing Node
  participant G as Cluster Bus

  N->>N: Start Valkey
  N->>N: Check nodes.conf
  N->>N: No prior cluster memory
  O->>E: Trigger introduction
  E->>N: CLUSTER MEET
  G->>N: Share topology
  O->>N: Assign role and attach if replica






The important idea is that “pod running” is not the same as “node joined.” Without preserved cluster memory, the pod does not automatically count as the same logical member, even if the container name looks familiar.
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: The Living Cluster
text:
The Return With Memory
When the pod restarts with persistent storage and nodes.conf intact, the return path is much smoother.

the pod may come back on a new IP
nodes.conf restores the previous node ID
peers recognize the same member reconnecting
the cluster updates the current address for that identity






sequenceDiagram
  participant R as Restarted Pod
  participant P as PVC
  participant E as Existing Nodes
  participant G as Cluster Gossip

  R->>P: Read nodes.conf and local data
  P-->>R: Return existing node identity
  R->>E: Reconnect to known peers
  E->>G: Spread new address if needed
  G->>E: Refresh cluster view
  R->>R: Resume as same logical member






This is why persistent storage is not just about bytes. It is also about preserving cluster identity.
That difference is one of the central lessons of the whole booklet: a restarted container is not automatically the same cluster member unless the right memory survives with it.
crumbs: 9  The Living Cluster

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Operator Reconcile Loop
This appendix keeps the most important flows in a tighter technical form. The order of events is intentionally direct here.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
The operator reads the custom resource and related child resources.
The API server returns the current spec, status, and object state.
Watch events notify the operator when something changes.
The operator compares desired state with actual state.
If a mismatch exists, the operator reconciles by creating, updating, or deleting resources.
The operator updates status through the API server.
The API server persists the new state in etcd.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Basic Operator Creation Flow

Create a local cluster such as kind create cluster --name valkey-operator-doc.
Run kubebuilder init.
Run kubebuilder create api.
Edit api/v1alpha1/valkey_types.go.
Edit internal/controller/valkey_controller.go.
Run make manifests generate fmt.
Run go build ./... and make test.
Install the CRD with make install.
Run the manager.
Apply the sample custom resource with kubectl apply -f config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_valkey.yaml.
Verify that the controller creates the child Service and Deployment.
Verify that the custom resource reaches status.phase=Ready.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Scratch-Built Operator Flow

Apply the ValkeyCluster custom resource.
The API server validates the request.
The object is stored in etcd.
The controller receives the new desired state through the API server watch.
The controller creates the ConfigMap.
The controller creates the headless Services.
The controller creates the client-facing Service.
The controller creates the StatefulSet objects.
If shard anti-affinity is enabled, the controller adds the annotation to the pod template.
The API server validates and stores those child resources.
The StatefulSet controller creates Pod objects.
The API server admission chain handles each pod create request.
If the annotation is present, the mutating webhook injects the shard anti-affinity rules.
The scheduler assigns the pod to a node.
The kubelet starts the pod.
Operator-managed bootstrap logic introduces nodes to each other.
Primaries receive slots.
Replicas are attached to their intended primary.
Cluster status is checked and the cluster is marked ready.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Official Operator Runtime

The CRDs are already installed in the cluster.
The user applies ValkeyCluster.
The API server validates the object.
The object is stored in etcd.
The operator receives the watch event and starts reconcile.
The operator creates cluster-level resources such as Services, ConfigMaps, and auth-related objects.
Those resources are validated and stored in etcd.
The operator calculates desired topology from shards and replicas.
The operator creates one internal ValkeyNode object per required member.
The API server stores those ValkeyNode objects.
The ValkeyNode controller creates the actual workloads from those internal objects.
The API server stores the workload objects.
The workload controllers create Pods.
The scheduler assigns nodes.
The kubelet starts containers and updates pod status.
The operator observes readiness and pod IPs.
The operator reads live Valkey state through network calls such as CLUSTER INFO and CLUSTER NODES.
If isolated nodes exist, the operator introduces them with CLUSTER MEET.
The operator assigns slots to primaries.
The operator attaches replicas to primaries.
The operator verifies shard count, replica count, and slot coverage.
The operator updates ValkeyCluster.status.
The API server stores the updated status in etcd.
The same reconcile pattern continues for scale, restart recovery, and topology drift.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Official Operator Persistence Direction

Persistence is declared on ValkeyCluster.
The ValkeyCluster controller copies that persistence block into each ValkeyNode.
The ValkeyNode controller reconciles a named PVC for the node.
The PVC becomes a first-class child resource instead of hiding inside volumeClaimTemplates.
The pod mounts that PVC.
The node stores persistent data and nodes.conf there.
Status conditions report whether the PVC is ready and whether the requested size is satisfied.
Reclaim policy and finalizers control retain-vs-delete behavior on node deletion.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Official Operator Placement Direction

Placement intent is declared on ValkeyCluster.
The controller propagates that intent to each ValkeyNode.
The ValkeyNode resource builder augments topology spread constraints with shard-aware labels.
The workload pod template contains the final scheduling rules.
The scheduler places nodes using those native Kubernetes constraints.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Client Routing Flow

The client connects to a startup node or API layer.
The startup node returns the current slot map.
The client caches the slot-to-node mapping.
The client sends the command to the primary that owns the target slot.
If topology changed, the client receives redirect information.
The client refreshes the slot map and retries against the new owner.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Headless Service Identity Flow

The StatefulSet creates a pod such as valkey-0.
The headless Service publishes a DNS record for that exact pod.
Peers and the operator use that DNS name to address the specific member.
If the pod restarts, the IP may change but the DNS identity pattern remains stable.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
New Pod Join

A pod starts and opens the client and cluster-bus ports.
Valkey checks for nodes.conf.
No prior cluster memory is found.
An operator or script triggers the introduction flow.
An existing node performs CLUSTER MEET.
Topology information spreads across the cluster bus.
The new member is assigned its role.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Restart With PVC

A pod restarts and the same PVC is attached again.
Valkey reads nodes.conf and local cluster data.
The previous node ID is recovered.
The pod reconnects to known peers.
If the pod IP changed, peers update the current address for that node ID.
Replication catches up if needed.
The member resumes as the same logical node.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Restart Without PVC

A pod restarts without the files that preserve identity.
nodes.conf is missing.
A new node ID is generated.
The pod has no local knowledge of the old cluster.
Automatic rejoin does not happen.
An operator or script must reintroduce the node.
Role assignment and topology sync happen again.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Leader Failure and Failover

A client sends traffic to the current slot owner.
The leader becomes unavailable.
Gossip spreads failure information across the cluster bus.
A replica is promoted according to cluster state.
Slot ownership changes to the new primary.
Clients refresh routing and start sending traffic to the promoted node.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

title: Appendix: System Replays
text:
Secret Delivery

A Secret is created or updated through the Kubernetes API.
The API server validates and stores it in etcd.
The kubelet for the assigned node requests the Secret.
The Secret is delivered to the node.
The pod receives it as files or environment variables.
The application uses it for passwords, ACLs, or TLS material.
crumbs: 10  Appendix: System Replays

text:

This appendix points to the repositories and design threads behind the work discussed in this booklet.
